desc: The princess of Greek myth, chained to a rock as a sacrifice and rescued by Perseus.
Home to the Andromeda Galaxy (M31) — the nearest large spiral and the most distant object visible to the naked eye.
An ancient zodiac constellation, the Water Bearer pouring a stream that flows toward the Southern Fish.
Faint but vast, riding the ecliptic so the Moon and planets pass through it.
The eagle that carried the thunderbolts of Zeus. Its brightest star Altair is one corner of the Summer Triangle.
Straddles the celestial equator along the bright star clouds of the Milky Way.
The charioteer, often shown carrying a goat and her kids. Capella, its brilliant lead star, is the sixth-brightest in the sky.
A rich Milky Way constellation packed with open star clusters.
The herdsman driving the bears around the pole. Its kite-shaped figure is anchored by Arcturus, the brightest star in the northern sky.
Find it by following the arc of the Big Dipper's handle — ‘arc to Arcturus’.
The faintest of the zodiac constellations, the crab crushed underfoot by Hercules.
Holds the Beehive Cluster (M44), a glittering naked-eye swarm of stars.
The hunting dogs of Boötes the herdsman.
Home to the Whirlpool Galaxy (M51), the first galaxy seen to have spiral structure.
Orion's larger hunting dog, marked by Sirius — the brightest star in the entire night sky.
Low in the winter evening sky, blazing below and to the left of Orion.
The keel of the great ship Argo. Canopus, its leader, is the second-brightest star in the sky.
Contains the vast Carina Nebula and the once-rivalrous variable star Eta Carinae.
The vain queen, condemned to circle the pole forever. Her unmistakable ‘W’ (or ‘M’) of five bright stars never sets from mid-northern latitudes.
Sits in a glittering stretch of the Milky Way full of star clusters.
The wise centaur Chiron. Its brightest star, Alpha Centauri, is the closest star system to the Sun (4.4 light-years).
Hosts Omega Centauri, the largest and brightest globular cluster in the sky.
The sea monster sent to devour Andromeda. One of the largest constellations in the sky.
Home to Mira, the first variable star discovered, which fades and brightens over ~11 months.
The flowing hair of Queen Berenice, sacrificed to the gods.
A window out of the galactic plane, dense with distant galaxies and the Coma Cluster.
A graceful semicircle of stars — the crown given to Ariadne and set in the sky.
Holds the ‘Blaze Star’ T CrB, a recurrent nova that flares roughly every 80 years.
The smallest constellation in the sky, yet one of the most famous — the Southern Cross, a beacon for navigators below the equator.
Its long axis points toward the south celestial pole. Beside it lies the dark Coalsack Nebula.
The swan flying down the length of the Milky Way, also known as the Northern Cross. Deneb, its tail, marks a corner of the Summer Triangle.
Threaded with nebulae — the North America, Veil and Pelican — and the famous black hole Cygnus X-1.
A small southern constellation that holds most of the Large Magellanic Cloud — a satellite galaxy of the Milky Way.
Within it lies the Tarantula Nebula, the most active star-forming region known in the Local Group.
The dragon coiling around the north pole between the two Bears.
Thuban, its faint star, was the pole star of the ancient Egyptians who aligned the pyramids to it.
A long, winding river of stars — the second-longest constellation, meandering from near Orion deep into the southern sky.
It ends at Achernar, ‘the river's end’, a rapidly spinning, flattened star.
The twins Castor and Pollux, marked by the two bright stars that bear their names.
A zodiac constellation; the radiant of the reliable Geminid meteor shower each December.
The kneeling hero of the Twelve Labours, the fifth-largest constellation.
Its Keystone asterism frames M13, the finest globular cluster in the northern sky.
The largest constellation in the sky, a vast water-snake winding over a quarter of the way around the heavens.
Its lone bright star Alphard means ‘the solitary one’.
A crouching lion and one of the few constellations that truly resembles its namesake. Its head and mane form the backwards-question-mark Sickle.
A zodiac constellation on the ecliptic; the Leonid meteor shower radiates from here each November.
The only zodiac constellation representing an object rather than a creature — the scales of justice held by neighbouring Virgo.
Its star names mean the ‘northern’ and ‘southern claw’, a memory of when these stars belonged to Scorpius.
A faint, sprawling constellation said to need the eyes of a lynx to see.
The lyre of Orpheus. Tiny but brilliant, led by Vega — the fifth-brightest star and a corner of the Summer Triangle.
Holds the Ring Nebula (M57), a glowing smoke-ring cast off by a dying star.
A faint unicorn set among the bright winter stars, crossed by the Milky Way.
Contains the Rosette Nebula and the Christmas Tree Cluster.
The constellation of the south celestial pole — its faint star Sigma Octantis is the southern equivalent of Polaris.
The healer Asclepius wrestling a great serpent — the ‘13th sign’ of the zodiac, which the Sun passes through each year.
Holds Barnard's Star, the fastest-moving star across our sky.
One of the most recognisable constellations in the sky — the Hunter striding with club and shield. Its brightest stars form a vast hourglass anchored by the three-star Belt.
It straddles the celestial equator, so it is visible from nearly everywhere on Earth, dominating winter evenings.
The winged horse sprung from the blood of Medusa. Its body is the Great Square of Pegasus, a huge signpost of the autumn sky.
Home to 51 Pegasi, the first Sun-like star found to host an exoplanet.
The hero who slew Medusa, holding her severed head — marked by Algol, the ‘Demon Star’, which visibly dims every 2.9 days.
Radiant of the Perseid meteor shower and home to the spectacular Double Cluster.
A faint southern constellation; its star Beta Pictoris hosts a famous dusty disk and a directly-imaged planet.
Two fish tied together by their tails, swimming in opposite directions — a faint zodiac constellation.
Contains the vernal equinox, where the Sun crosses the celestial equator each spring.
The southern fish drinking the stream poured by Aquarius. Its lone bright star, Fomalhaut, gleams alone in a barren patch of autumn sky.
Fomalhaut hosts a ring of debris and was an early target in the hunt for exoplanets.
The third-smallest constellation — a tiny, convincing little arrow shot across the Milky Way.
The archer-centaur aiming his bow at Scorpius. Its bright stars form the ‘Teapot’ asterism.
Marks the direction of the centre of our galaxy — its star clouds and nebulae are the richest patch of the entire Milky Way.
A rare constellation that looks like its namesake — a scorpion with a curving tail and a red heart, Antares, a vast supergiant whose name means ‘rival of Mars’.
The scorpion that, in myth, killed Orion; the two never share the sky.
A small constellation containing the dazzling Wild Duck Cluster (M11), one of the richest open clusters known.
The only constellation split in two — the serpent's head (Caput) and tail (Cauda), held by Ophiuchus between them.
Home to the Eagle Nebula (M16) and its ‘Pillars of Creation’.
The charging bull of the zodiac, its face a V-shaped cluster (the Hyades) with the orange eye Aldebaran.
Carries the Pleiades (M45) on its shoulder and the Crab Nebula (M1), a supernova seen in 1054.
A simple, slender triangle of three stars.
Holds the Triangulum Galaxy (M33), the third-largest in our Local Group and a faint naked-eye target under dark skies.
A southern bird constellation that holds the Small Magellanic Cloud and the magnificent globular cluster 47 Tucanae.
The Great Bear, containing the Big Dipper (or Plough) — the most famous star pattern in the northern sky. Its two end stars point to Polaris.
Third-largest of all constellations, home to many bright galaxies including M81 and M101.
The Little Bear, whose tail-tip is Polaris — the North Star, which sits almost exactly above the north pole and barely moves all night.
The Little Dipper hangs from Polaris, a ready-made clock and compass for the northern sky.
The sails of the ship Argo. Contains the Vela supernova remnant and a famous pulsar.
Part of the giant ‘False Cross’ sometimes mistaken for the Southern Cross.
The second-largest constellation, a maiden holding an ear of wheat marked by the brilliant blue star Spica.
Its bowl brims with thousands of galaxies — the heart of the Virgo Cluster.
Follow the Dipper's arc to Arcturus, then ‘spike to Spica’.
A faint fox below Cygnus, home to the Dumbbell Nebula (M27) — the first planetary nebula ever discovered.
